summary: American doctrine moved from heavily offensive torpedo ships toward all-round destroyers able to defend a Pacific fleet and its logistics.
body: Earlier U.S. designs could carry very heavy torpedo batteries, but that concentration reinforced their offensive character and created vulnerability. The Sims pattern pushed the force toward a more balanced fleet escort, although anti-submarine warfare received less institutional attention than in the Royal Navy before the United States entered the war. Pacific operations still demanded durable all-round destroyers to screen combat forces and their supply train across ocean distances.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts offensive torpedo emphasis with defensive fleet and logistics requirements.

title: Judge destroyers by mission fit, not a universal ranking
videoTitle: Patreon 50: 1939 Destroyer Doctrine of the combatant navies & Where did it come from?
summary: The final argument rejects a universal ‘best destroyer’: a good force has enough ships, a doctrine it can execute, and designs matched to its geography and fleet.
body: World War I experience informed every navy, but it could also be misapplied when a local lesson was projected onto a wider war. Dr. Clarke stresses that real doctrine must work with available ships rather than an ideal paper fleet. Destroyers are not incidental to capital-ship operations: their torpedoes, smoke, screening, and close attack can force a larger opponent to maneuver, disrupt its fire, and abandon attacks on convoys or other vulnerable targets.

evidence.note: The conclusion relates doctrine, force size, mission fit, and destroyer influence on capital ships.

title: Cressy at the turn from Victorian to modern cruisers
videoTitle: Cressy(1899) Class: Famous for the Wrong Things - 19th Century Cruisers Series 2 Part 12
summary: The Cressy class is placed between late Victorian armoured cruisers and the faster-changing cruiser designs of the twentieth century.
body: Dr. Clarke presents the class as both the end of a nineteenth-century design lineage and a starting point for later British armoured cruisers. William White's methodical design process produced credible ships for the requirements understood in the late 1890s, but rapid changes in propulsion, weapons, and tactics made that transitional position unforgiving.

evidence.note: The transcript calls the Cressys the last nineteenth-century cruisers and a starting point for twentieth-century cruiser development, then explains White's deliberate design process.

title: Why the Royal Navy wanted the Cressy class
videoTitle: Cressy(1899) Class: Famous for the Wrong Things - 19th Century Cruisers Series 2 Part 12
summary: White's requirements combine fleet support, detached commerce protection, long coal endurance, and enough fighting power to meet foreign first-class cruisers.
body: The ships were intended to reinforce battleships in close action, protect communications on detached service, and remain competitive with foreign cruisers. Their large coal allowance and sustained-speed calculations reveal how endurance shaped a worldwide cruiser, while their place beside battleships shows that the design was also meant to add numbers and tactical pressure in a fleet action.

evidence.note: White's design statement lists fleet, detached-service, commerce-protection, speed, and coal-endurance requirements.
The transcript explains how armoured cruisers could support a battleship line and complicate an opponent's tactical problem.

title: Cressy armament and the cost of low casemates
videoTitle: Cressy(1899) Class: Famous for the Wrong Things - 19th Century Cruisers Series 2 Part 12
summary: The class carried a formidable 9.2-inch and six-inch battery, but much of its broadside could be masked by heavy seas.
body: The Cressys concentrated their strength on the broadside, with the 9.2-inch gun supplying a much heavier blow than the six-inch weapons. Four of the six six-inch guns on each side sat low enough to become difficult to use in rough water, a limitation tied to expectations of Channel and Mediterranean service rather than South Atlantic or North Sea conditions.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the broadside arrangement, low casemates in heavy seas, and the shell-weight difference between the six-inch and 9.2-inch guns.

title: How the Admiralty enforced class standardization
videoTitle: Cressy(1899) Class: Famous for the Wrong Things - 19th Century Cruisers Series 2 Part 12
summary: Different private yards built the class, but Admiralty officials enforced common boilers, machinery standards, and internal arrangements.
body: Dr. Clarke contrasts British construction control with the greater variation found in some contemporary foreign classes. The Director of Naval Construction, Third Sea Lord, and senior engineering authority could withhold acceptance and future contracts, giving the Admiralty powerful leverage to make civilian yards deliver ships to a standardized pattern.

evidence.note: The transcript explains common machinery requirements, official sign-off, payment leverage, and the threat of withholding later naval contracts.

title: The Cressys as working ships across the empire
videoTitle: Cressy(1899) Class: Famous for the Wrong Things - 19th Century Cruisers Series 2 Part 12
summary: Class careers included China and West Indies stations, training, battleship escort, convoy work, and support to the Harwich Force at Heligoland Bight.
body: The service histories show that these were not simply obsolete ships waiting for disaster: they carried out imperial station work, protected a damaged battleship on passage, trained sailors, and undertook convoy duties. In August 1914 the Seventh Cruiser Squadron helped deter further German intervention at Heligoland Bight and assisted damaged British forces, illustrating the useful fleet-support role for which the class had been conceived.

evidence.note: The transcript follows Cressy and Sutlej through overseas stations, reserve, training, convoy, and escort duties.
The transcript covers Aboukir and Hogue with the Seventh Cruiser Squadron and their support after Heligoland Bight.

title: Bacchante and Euryalus at Gallipoli
videoTitle: Cressy(1899) Class: Famous for the Wrong Things - 19th Century Cruisers Series 2 Part 12
summary: The surviving Cressys supported landings and bombarded Ottoman positions before continuing with Red Sea and Indian Ocean duties.
body: Bacchante maneuvered close to shore to improve her fire against Ottoman guns, supported Anzac Cove, and helped suppress artillery during major attacks. Euryalus carried troops for the Cape Helles landings, provided naval gunfire, and later served in Egypt, the Red Sea, and farther east, demonstrating the class's continued value in expeditionary and regional operations.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Bacchante's Gallipoli gunfire support and later service.
The transcript describes Euryalus carrying landing troops, bombarding Ottoman positions, and serving in Egypt, the Red Sea, and Asia.

title: How U-9 sank Aboukir, Hogue, and Cressy
videoTitle: Cressy(1899) Class: Famous for the Wrong Things - 19th Century Cruisers Series 2 Part 12
summary: Weather separated the armoured cruisers from their destroyer screen before U-9 torpedoed all three during rescue attempts on 22 September 1914.
body: The Seventh Cruiser Squadron belonged to a combined force that included destroyers and submarines, but heavy weather broke up the screen and Admiralty orders kept the cruisers on patrol. Aboukir's first hit was mistaken for a mine; Hogue and Cressy then closed to rescue survivors and became targets in turn. The episode explains both the tactical sequence and why early-war officers, with little evidence of submarine success and few anti-submarine tools, misjudged the new danger.

evidence.note: The transcript sets out the Southern Force, patrol orders, weather separation, and the limited early-war evidence about submarine effectiveness.
The transcript narrates U-9's successive attacks, rescue efforts, losses, and the absence of mature anti-submarine weapons.

title: The Cressy disaster and Britain's missing medium cruiser
videoTitle: Cressy(1899) Class: Famous for the Wrong Things - 19th Century Cruisers Series 2 Part 12
summary: The class's loss is treated as a failure of escort and force structure, not proof that its 1890s design had always been worthless.
body: Dr. Clarke argues that judging the ships solely by a submarine threat barely imaginable when they were designed turns hindsight into a false design requirement. Their immediate need in 1914 was a destroyer screen, while the wider fleet problem was Britain's emphasis on battlecruisers and light cruisers without enough modern medium armoured cruisers. That gap also explains why a Cressy would not have solved Cradock's problem at Coronel, where newer, faster and better-armed ships were required.

evidence.note: The transcript rejects anachronistic submarine-proofing expectations and argues that the cruisers needed destroyer escorts and a stronger medium tier.
The transcript explains why Cressy was unsuitable for Coronel and advocates continued production of later medium armoured cruisers.

title: Sims turns carriers into offensive weapons
videoTitle: Striking First: The Development of US Carrier Naval Aviation Doctrine by War Gaming & Fleet Problems
summary: The lecture identifies William Sims and Naval War College wargaming as the starting point for US carrier aviation as an offensive doctrine.
body: Dr. Clarke frames Sims as the key figure who used the Naval War College to push carrier aviation beyond spotting for battleships. The important doctrine is simple and durable: find and hit the opposing carrier first, and launch the largest air group quickly enough to make that first blow decisive. That argument gives viewers a through-line from early interwar exercises to later US fleet-carrier design priorities.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Sims as Dr. Clarke's candidate for the originator of US naval aviation doctrine.
The transcript says Sims returned to the Naval War College and used wargaming to show carriers as strike assets.
The transcript states the first-carrier-hit and largest-fastest-launch criteria for US carrier doctrine.

title: Fleet Problems simulate carriers before carriers arrive
videoTitle: Striking First: The Development of US Carrier Naval Aviation Doctrine by War Gaming & Fleet Problems
summary: Fleet Problems One and Two use battleships as simulated carriers, letting the US Navy test air strikes before it has real fleet carriers in service.
body: This watch point shows the US Navy treating carrier aviation as a practical fleet problem before it has the hardware to match the idea. New York and Oklahoma are assigned carrier roles, and a single aircraft represents a larger strike that attacks the Gatun Dam. Dr. Clarke uses the example to connect the 1923 exercises to later confidence in carrier raids and long-range strike effects.

evidence.note: The transcript says Fleet Problems One and Two used USS New York and USS Oklahoma as simulated aircraft carriers.
The transcript describes the Panama exercise, Langley modeling, and the simulated Gatun Dam attack.
The transcript links the exercises to faith in strike capability and later 1942 carrier raids.

title: Reeves turns war-game ideas into deck practice
videoTitle: Striking First: The Development of US Carrier Naval Aviation Doctrine by War Gaming & Fleet Problems
summary: Joseph Reeves, Langley, and Moffett show the US Navy moving from carrier theory into sortie rates, aircraft range, and aviation career structures.
body: Dr. Clarke presents Reeves as the officer who turns much of Sims's theory into practical carrier work. Aboard Langley, Reeves confronts the gap between what wargaming says carriers should do and what early aircraft, decks, and procedures can actually deliver. Moffett and the Bureau of Aeronautics matter because doctrine also needs institutions, career paths, and senior advocates if it is going to survive inside the wider Navy.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Reeves as a father of US naval aviation who turns Sims's theories into practice.
The transcript says Reeves found Langley did not fit what the Navy wanted and worked on takeoff intervals and carrier capability.
The transcript says US theoretical work often outpaced real systems while officers understood range and strike potential.
The transcript describes Moffett as the institutional figure bringing strands of naval aviation together.

title: Lexington and Saratoga prove battle and raiding doctrine
videoTitle: Striking First: The Development of US Carrier Naval Aviation Doctrine by War Gaming & Fleet Problems
summary: The arrival of Lexington and Saratoga lets the US Navy test carrier-versus-carrier battles, Pacific threats, and fast raiding strikes over wide distances.
body: Once Lexington and Saratoga enter service, the Fleet Problems can treat carriers as central actors rather than abstractions. Dr. Clarke separates two linked ideas: battle doctrine, which aims to hit the enemy carrier first, and raiding doctrine, which uses carrier mobility to strike far-apart targets and force bad defensive choices. The Pacific setting matters because the US Navy is already thinking about Japanese carrier capability and wide-ocean maneuver.

evidence.note: The transcript says Lexington and Saratoga entered service in 1927 and became linchpins of Fleet Problems.
The transcript says real-world exercises often focused on a Japanese threat and carrier inclination.
The transcript distinguishes battle doctrine from raiding doctrine.
The transcript explains how moving carriers and strike range create dilemmas across hundreds of nautical miles.

title: Treaty limits collide with large-carrier doctrine
videoTitle: Striking First: The Development of US Carrier Naval Aviation Doctrine by War Gaming & Fleet Problems
summary: A treaty-era discussion explains why the US Navy wants more Lexington-like carriers but keeps running into cumulative tonnage limits.
body: This section ties aircraft speed, carrier vulnerability, and treaty limits together. Dr. Clarke argues that US doctrine wants large carriers able to generate big strikes, but the treaty system prevents the Navy from simply building six or eight Lexington-sized ships. The result is a recurring procurement tension: smaller carriers multiply decks, yet they do not efficiently produce the aircraft volume the doctrine demands.

evidence.note: The transcript says US naval aviation doctrine evolved as technology and the strategic situation changed.
The transcript contrasts early fighter interception with later carrier-defense problems and British operating conditions.
The transcript says treaty cumulative tonnage limits blocked building enough Lexington-like carriers.
The transcript says the US Navy disliked small carriers because they did not generate enough aircraft or strikes.

title: Carrier exercises expose first-blow and Pearl Harbor risks
videoTitle: Striking First: The Development of US Carrier Naval Aviation Doctrine by War Gaming & Fleet Problems
summary: Lexington-Saratoga maneuvers and Pearl Harbor attack exercises show the US Navy understanding both carrier first-strike power and forward-base vulnerability.
body: Dr. Clarke uses a 1931 carrier duel to show how quickly a carrier battle can turn once scouting and strike timing line up. The same section moves to US exercises against Pearl Harbor, noting that the vulnerability of the base was demonstrated before the Japanese attack. The point is not that commanders had perfect answers, but that reconnaissance, supplies, aircraft readiness, and forward-base logistics shaped the choices available to them.

evidence.note: The transcript describes a 1931 Lexington-Saratoga exercise where Saratoga's first strike sank Lexington.
The transcript says the exercise caused calls for smaller carriers but also highlighted fixed carrier systems and aircraft capacity.
The transcript describes 1932 and 1938 exercises attacking Pearl Harbor and says senior officers knew the risk.
The transcript explains Kimmel's maritime-patrol dilemma as a supply and readiness problem at a forward base.

title: Wargaming culture reinforces large US carriers
videoTitle: Striking First: The Development of US Carrier Naval Aviation Doctrine by War Gaming & Fleet Problems
summary: A comparison of US, Royal Navy, and Japanese wargaming leads into why Yorktown, Ranger, Wasp, and Hornet reveal the US preference for larger carriers.
body: This watch point compares how different navies used wargaming to test assumptions under treaty and strategic pressure. Dr. Clarke argues that the US Naval War College's questioning culture gave the Navy an edge, while the Royal Navy's games highlighted resource limits and Japanese games could be more constrained by confidence and expectation. That setup explains the design lesson: Ranger and Wasp can be used, but Yorktown and Hornet better match the US desire for large air groups and sustained strike generation.

evidence.note: The transcript says the US Navy's integration of wargaming through the Naval War College gave it an edge.
The transcript contrasts Japanese wargaming culture and confidence with the US questioning habit.
The transcript says Royal Navy wargames showed that Britain lacked resources for a three-nation war.
The transcript says Yorktown and the other large carriers show US operational needs, while Wasp is a smaller compromise.

title: Essex production scales the doctrine while exposing its limits
videoTitle: Striking First: The Development of US Carrier Naval Aviation Doctrine by War Gaming & Fleet Problems
summary: The Essex class, kamikaze gap, fighter tactics, combat air patrol, and Reeves's questions show US carrier doctrine expanding without becoming omniscient.
body: Dr. Clarke treats the Essex program as the industrial expression of the US doctrine: many large carriers able to launch large air groups quickly. The doctrine works within its intended scenario, but it does not anticipate everything, including kamikaze attacks. The closing lesson separates doctrine from tactics: the doctrine explains why the Navy wants air control and massed carrier aviation, while combat air patrol, fighter direction, and tactical devices evolve as technology and wartime experience catch up.

evidence.note: The transcript says the Essex class was planned at 32 ships with 24 completed and gives early build timing.
The transcript says the US Navy lost and sank many carriers, and that kamikaze attacks were not factored into wargaming before they happened.
The transcript distinguishes doctrine from tactics and links the big blue blanket back to Sims's first-hit and fast-launch ideas.
The transcript explains radar, radio control, CAP development, and the difficulty of defining when a new concept begins.
The transcript closes with Reeves's questions about control of the air, scouting denial, and aircraft support to surface forces.

title: Northeast England's shipbuilding concentration
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The opening scale comparison shows why the northeast's four rivers were a strategic industrial resource.
body: Dr. Clarke notes that the northeast of England built roughly half the world's ships in the opening decade of the twentieth century. The important point is not hull output alone: component makers, docks, and supporting works made this a dense industrial system capable of sustaining naval power.

evidence.note: The transcript describes four northeast rivers building half the world's ships and their supporting infrastructure.

title: Cammell Laird's docks as industrial capacity
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Large docks, basins, and cranes show how a yard could build, fit out, and repair ships at scale.
body: Using Cammell Laird as an example, the video distinguishes assembly, fitting-out basins, dry docks, and repair space. Movable caissons could extend a usable dock length, while heavy cranes and multiple basins turned waterfront acreage into practical warship capacity.

evidence.note: Cammell Laird's docks, basins, caissons, cranes, and fitting-out work are described.

title: Why repair capacity matters after battle
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: A fleet's real strength depends on how many damaged ships its docks can restore at once.
body: The comparison after Jutland is deliberately logistical: the German Navy had fewer repair facilities, while the Royal Navy could send repairable ships into dock within days. A numerical fleet advantage is fragile if damaged ships queue for the same scarce dock space.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts German repair delays with Royal Navy dock availability after Jutland.

title: Natural harbours and battleship-sized docks
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: A viewer asks what makes natural and artificial harbours useful for naval infrastructure.
body: The answer stresses that dock dimensions alone are not enough: a battleship must also be able to reach the dock through a sufficiently deep approach. Britain benefited from many suitable sites, but investment and the ability to develop them mattered as much as geology.

evidence.note: The answer discusses natural harbours, depth, river access, and battleship-sized dry docks.

question: What makes the world's best natural or artificial harbours useful for major naval docks?
answerShort: Depth, approach routes, and the ability to build large docks matter alongside the harbour itself; a battleship-sized dock is useless if the ship cannot reach it.

title: Armstrong Whitworth as a heavy-engineering giant
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Armstrong Whitworth illustrates how a major yard combined long waterfront works with broad industrial capabilities.
body: The firm’s Tyne works stretched for almost two kilometres, a scale that is difficult to appreciate from a list of shipbuilders. Dr. Clarke uses later corporate comparisons to convey how a company producing ships, weapons, and engineering products could become strategically significant.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Armstrong Whitworth's Tyne works, merger with Vickers, and exceptional industrial scale.

title: Britain's coastal network and maritime investment
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The answer explains why Britain developed so many maritime facilities without treating geography as destiny.
body: Rivers and estuaries were useful, but the video argues that coastal trade, accumulated capital, and repeated investment made Britain’s port network unusually deep. Competition between facilities then encouraged further development rather than a single centrally planned system.

evidence.note: The answer links Britain’s geography, coastal movement of goods, investment, and port competition.

question: Did Britain's rivers and coastal geography effectively expand its maritime capacity?
answerShort: They helped, but Britain’s advantage also rested on coastal trade, money, and a long habit of investing in ports and dock infrastructure.

title: A cut-throat market of private yards
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Britain’s many competing builders made capacity resilient, but also exposed firms to failure.
body: The video argues that a monopoly did not emerge because demand could support several firms while competition remained fierce. Yards had to deliver quality ships to survive, and bankruptcy was a recurring feature rather than proof that shipbuilding capacity had disappeared.

evidence.note: The transcript explains why multiple British firms competed and sometimes failed.

title: Graving docks, floating docks, and ship lifts
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: A terminology question becomes a practical explanation of how different repair facilities support a hull.
body: A graving dock is a basin drained around a ship, whereas a floating dry dock lifts the vessel by submerging and rising beneath it. The answer also notes that names overlap in common usage and that slipways and ship lifts serve smaller vessels or different repair needs.

evidence.note: The answer defines graving docks, floating dry docks, and related lifting arrangements.

question: What is the difference between a graving dock and a dry dock?
answerShort: A graving dock is usually a drained fixed basin, while a floating dry dock lifts a vessel; everyday usage often calls both dry docks.

title: A global network for keeping ships operational
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The Royal Navy's overseas dock network made maintenance a strategic capability rather than a rear-area afterthought.
body: A navy that can build many battleships but cannot repair them soon has expensive inactive hulls. The discussion points to worldwide dock space and the possibility of towing a floating dock to distant stations such as the Falklands as ways to extend operational endurance.

evidence.note: The transcript makes repair and overseas docks central to sustaining a fleet.

title: Dock size as a constraint on ship design
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Ship dimensions follow the hard ceiling set by the facilities that must build and maintain them.
body: The video uses British dock expansion and the Queen Elizabeth class to show that infrastructure can shape hull length and construction choices for generations. Chatham’s declining utility is presented as the opposite case: once ship sizes outgrew its facilities, its strategic importance fell.

evidence.note: The transcript links dock dimensions to Queen Elizabeth class design and Chatham's decline.

title: Devonport's flow of routine and emergency repairs
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Connected basins and separate docks let Devonport sort ships by the urgency and duration of maintenance.
body: Dr. Clarke describes how through docks, basins, caissons, and alternate entrances support a practical repair workflow. Long-term work can be separated from routine servicing, while a clear lock dock provides a place to stabilize emergency cases before further repair.

evidence.note: Devonport's linked docks and repair allocation are explained.

title: Integrated yards could exchange engines to meet deadlines
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The Harlan and Wolff–Workman Clark example shows how nearby integrated yards could solve bottlenecks together.
body: The yards were not merely final assemblers buying in every component; they could build engines and much else themselves. When each yard was delayed on a different engine type, exchanging the units let both complete ships on schedule, revealing a dense local industrial ecosystem.

evidence.note: The transcript describes integrated production and the engine exchange between neighbouring yards.

title: Rosyth and the Grand Fleet's maintenance problem
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Rosyth exposes the risk of basing a major fleet where dock capacity lags operational demand.
body: The video contrasts the better-supported southern yards with the facilities available to the Grand Fleet in the north. With only limited dry-dock capacity and construction still incomplete, Rosyth was a weak prospect for wartime repair despite its operational location.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Rosyth's limited dock space and delayed construction.

title: Repair capacity as wartime force generation
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Repairing damaged ships reduced the replacement burden and helped the Royal Navy remain effective in a long war.
body: The argument is that dockyards make naval expansion possible by returning damaged ships to service instead of forcing new construction to replace every loss. Britain and the United States both needed time to expand their industrial systems, while Germany, Italy, and Japan faced harder trade-offs between repair and new construction.

evidence.note: The transcript connects repair capacity, replacement demand, and World War II fleet endurance.

title: Hong Kong as an imperial repair base
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Hong Kong's naval yard demonstrates how workshops, stores, basins, and a dry dock supported a fleet far from Britain.
body: The site is distinguished from nearby commercial yards because it was a dedicated naval facility with its own repair infrastructure and Admiralty stores. The video also frames Singapore's later growth as part of the same broader problem of sustaining forces in the Far East.

evidence.note: Hong Kong's naval yard, associated facilities, and Far East role are described.

title: Bermuda's floating dock as strategic mobility
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: Bermuda shows why a movable dock could be more useful politically and strategically than a permanent basin.
body: The Royal Navy used Bermuda's position to support the American and West Indies station, but its floating dock limited the size of ships it could support. The earlier dock was built for ironclads, towed out by warships, and offered the additional advantage that it could be moved if a permanent facility became politically troublesome.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Bermuda's location, floating dock limits, construction, towing, and strategic rationale.

title: How a floating dry dock balances and maintains itself
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The dock's ability to lift ships depended on careful ballast control, and it could be careened for its own upkeep.
body: The Bermuda dock could be rolled or pulled ashore so workers could clean and maintain its hull. Its flexibility came with risk: a floating dock must be carefully balanced, because a misplaced ship or uneven flooding can make the dock itself shift and become dangerous.

evidence.note: The transcript explains careening, self-maintenance, balancing, corrosion, and the consequences of movement.

title: Why dry docks become strategic targets
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: A question about mobile docks leads to the logic behind raiding and sabotaging repair infrastructure.
body: The answer treats docks as obvious targets because disabling them can immobilize a fleet without sinking its ships. It connects German and Italian interest in British facilities with British raids and demolition operations against enemy dockyards in occupied Europe.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses attacks on British, French, Dutch, Belgian, and other dock facilities.

question: Were enemy floating docks and repair facilities priority targets for naval planners?
answerShort: Yes. Destroying or disabling docks restricts an opponent's ability to repair warships, so planners repeatedly treated them as high-value targets.

title: Floating docks for forward fleet support
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: A range of floating-dock sizes let the Royal Navy support everything from small craft to battleships at forward stations.
body: The video surveys different lifting capacities, including docks for destroyers, cruisers, and battleships, and follows their movement between stations such as Reykjavík, Sydney, Bermuda, Singapore, and Scapa Flow. These were staffed technical facilities with their own crews and cranes, supplemented by repair personnel brought alongside when work was needed.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses dock capacities, deployments, crews, and forward repair roles.

title: Can a floating dock service another navy's ship?
videoTitle: Patreon 44  "More than just ships: Infrastructure - the yards that ruled the world"
summary: The final technical answer sets out the practical conditions for using a dock with an unfamiliar warship.
body: A floating dock can service a foreign vessel if its lifting capacity and physical dimensions are adequate. Dock operators still need the vessel's weight distribution, pressure points, and centre-of-mass information so it can be supported without structural damage.

evidence.note: The answer specifies capacity, pressure points, centre of mass, and repair personnel.

question: Could one navy's floating dry dock service ships from another navy?
answerShort: Yes, if the ship fits within the dock's capacity and operators understand its support points and balance requirements.
